name: Microbot Medical Inc.

profile.description: Microbot Medical is a medical device company focused on transforming endovascular procedures through advanced robotic technology. Its flagship LIBERTY Endovascular Robotic System is the first single-use, remotely operated robotic solution that allows physicians to conduct catheter-based procedures from outside the catheterization laboratory, avoiding radiation exposure and physical strain. The company also has NovaCross intraluminal revascularization technology and a collaboration with Emory University on autonomous robotic endovascular procedures.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Microbot achieved a transformative milestone with FDA clearance and full market release of the LIBERTY Endovascular Robotic System at the Society of Interventional Radiology (SIR) Annual Scientific Meeting in early 2026 — this represents a successful product launch that opens an entirely new revenue stream for a company that was pre-revenue since its 2010 founding [1]. AI Deployment (1/2): Microbot has a collaboration agreement with Emory University for the development of autonomous robotics in endovascular procedures, representing AI exploration with a credible academic partner, though no AI products are shipping yet [2]. Market Share (1/2): LIBERTY is the first single-use, remotely operated robotic solution for endovascular procedures, effectively creating a new category at the intersection of disposable medical devices and surgical robotics — though with zero current market share data available as commercialization just began [1]. Pipeline (1/2): NovaCross intraluminal revascularization technology provides a second product platform, and analyst forecasts project revenue growing from $4.39M in 2026 to $9.64M in 2027 (+120%) [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): As a pre-revenue company, R&D intensity cannot be meaningfully measured against a revenue base.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Microbot's stated purpose — 'transforming endovascular procedures through advanced robotic technology' — is clear, specific, and connects the company's work to tangible physician and patient benefits (reducing radiation exposure, physical strain, and cross-contamination risk) [2]. It differentiates from generic medical device missions but doesn't articulate a transformative societal vision beyond the product category. Community Impact (0/2): No specific charitable programs, community investments, or foundation activities were found in public materials. The company's 43-person team is focused on commercialization. Employee Retention (0/2): With 43 employees, no specific retention or turnover data are publicly available. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Harel Gadot is a co-founder who has led the company since inception and serves as Chairman — his decade-plus commitment through years of pre-revenue development demonstrates deep personal alignment with the mission [2]. The board approved a special CEO bonus recognizing milestones achieved, suggesting leadership-board alignment.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Microbot Medical's financial infrastructure reflects an early-stage medical device company transitioning from development to commercialization. D/E of 1.1% is exceptionally low, indicating near-zero debt and conservative capital structure [4]. However, ROE of -32.4% reflects continued pre-revenue losses, and Forward P/E of -7.1x indicates expected ongoing losses. Profit margin is 0% with no meaningful revenue yet — analysts project $4.39M in 2026 revenue from the LIBERTY launch, growing to $9.64M in 2027 [1]. The PEG ratio of 0.7x (below the 1.0 target) is the sole bright spot, suggesting high expected growth relative to valuation. Net loss of -$13.1M in the trailing period reflects ongoing R&D and commercialization spending. Liquidity passes the floor at $5.0M daily dollar volume. The infrastructure score benefits from the near-zero debt and attractive PEG ratio, but is dragged down by negative earnings, negative ROE, and zero profit margin.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): Microbot Medical's core business — manufacturing and selling physical robotic surgical systems — involves hardware-intensive products with FDA regulatory clearance requirements that create meaningful barriers to AI-only disruption [1]. The LIBERTY system is a physical robot that must be manufactured, sterilized, and deployed in catheterization labs — AI cannot substitute for the hardware. However, the endovascular robotics space sits directly adjacent to autonomous AI-driven surgical systems, where larger competitors like Intuitive Surgical (da Vinci) and Siemens Healthineers (Corindus) are investing heavily in AI-enhanced capabilities. If autonomous AI-guided catheter navigation advances rapidly, MBOT's human-operated remote robotics approach could face obsolescence pressure from AI-native competitors with greater resources. AI Opportunity (1/1): The collaboration with Emory University on autonomous robotics in endovascular procedures positions Microbot to integrate AI capabilities into future LIBERTY iterations [2]. While no AI revenue is quantifiable today, the autonomous robotics R&D represents direct AI product development.

infinite_game.summary: Microbot Medical demonstrates genuine infinite game characteristics through founder-CEO Harel Gadot's decade-plus commitment to building robotic technology that protects physicians and improves patient outcomes — a cause validated by years of pre-revenue perseverance. However, as a small, recently commercializing company with a single shipping product, the organization hasn't yet been tested by the strategic pivots, market crises, or competitive pressures that distinguish truly infinite players from promising startups.

just_cause.analysis: Microbot's purpose — protecting physicians from radiation exposure and physical strain while improving endovascular procedure outcomes — connects the company's work to a genuine human need that transcends quarterly earnings (2/3 pts). Founder-CEO Gadot has demonstrated sustained commitment to this cause through 16 years of pre-revenue development since the company's 2010 founding [2]. The mission shaped the LIBERTY system's design priorities (remote operation, single-use sterility, radiation protection). However, the cause hasn't been tested through leadership transitions, market crises, or multi-decade consistency to warrant a full 3/3.

trusting_teams.analysis: Microbot's 43-person team operates under a founder-led structure that has been strengthened with recent commercial leadership hires — including experienced healthcare officers added specifically for the LIBERTY launch (1/2 pts) [2]. CEO Gadot's statement about building 'an experienced commercial leadership team' and the company's emphasis on 'strong quality culture supporting cross-functional teams' suggest intentional organizational development. However, with such a small team and no specific retention data, evidence of exceptional trust is limited.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Microbot operates in the surgical robotics space alongside formidable competitors including Intuitive Surgical ($180B+ market cap), Siemens Healthineers (Corindus), and Johnson & Johnson (Monarch), whose scale and resources push Microbot to differentiate on disposability, cost, and radiation protection (1/1 pt). The company's decision to pursue single-use robotics rather than competing head-to-head with da Vinci's capital equipment model reflects learning from competitors' strengths and finding a differentiated position.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Microbot is a young company that has not yet faced the existential flexibility test — the company has been focused on developing and launching LIBERTY since inception and is only now beginning commercialization (0/2 pts). The NovaCross platform provides a second product vector, and the Emory University autonomous robotics collaboration suggests openness to AI-driven evolution. However, no evidence of transformative strategic pivots exists — the company has pursued the same endovascular robotics vision since founding.

courage_to_lead.analysis: CEO Gadot's decision to persist through 16 years of pre-revenue development — burning cash while building a medical robotics platform from scratch — demonstrates meaningful courage, particularly for a small-cap company competing against multibillion-dollar incumbents (1/2 pts) [2]. The choice to pursue single-use robotic design rather than the established capital equipment model represents a contrarian bet against industry convention. However, the company has not yet faced the test of choosing between short-term earnings and long-term investment, as it has had no earnings to sacrifice.

investment_thesis: Microbot Medical offers early-stage exposure to the endovascular robotics revolution through LIBERTY, the first single-use, remotely operated robotic system for catheter-based procedures. The full market release at SIR 2026 marks the transition from development-stage to commercial company, with analysts projecting $4.39M in 2026 revenue growing 120% to $9.64M in 2027 [1]. The addressable market is large — over 2 million endovascular procedures are performed annually in the U.S. alone, and LIBERTY's single-use design eliminates the capital equipment barrier that limits adoption of competing systems. At a $157M market cap with near-zero debt (D/E 1.1%) and a PEG ratio of 0.7x, the valuation implies significant growth potential if commercialization gains traction. The Emory University autonomous robotics collaboration adds an AI-driven product evolution pathway [2].
thesis_continued: The bear case is dominated by execution risk and capital constraints. Microbot has generated zero revenue in its 16-year history, lost $13.1M in the trailing period, and carries negative ROE of -32.4% — the transition from development to commercial revenue is the highest-risk inflection point for any medical device company [4]. The company must compete against Intuitive Surgical ($180B+ market cap), Siemens Healthineers, and J&J's Monarch platform with only 43 employees and a $157M market cap. Medical device adoption cycles are notoriously slow — hospital procurement committees, physician training requirements, and insurance reimbursement negotiations can delay revenue ramps for years. Additional capital raises will likely be needed, creating dilution risk. Forward P/E of -7.1x confirms the market expects continued losses. If LIBERTY adoption is slower than projected or competitors launch competing single-use solutions, the equity could face significant downside from current levels.
key_catalysts: LIBERTY commercial ramp — initial hospital installations and case volumes in 2026 will provide the first real-world adoption data, with the transition from $0 to $4.39M in projected revenue representing proof of commercial viability [1]

Insurance reimbursement codes — securing CPT codes and favorable reimbursement rates for LIBERTY-assisted procedures would remove a major adoption barrier and accelerate hospital procurement decisions

Autonomous robotics advancement — the Emory University collaboration on AI-driven autonomous endovascular procedures could yield next-generation LIBERTY capabilities that differentiate against larger competitors' manual systems [2]
key_risks: Commercial execution failure — the transition from FDA clearance to meaningful hospital adoption is the highest-mortality stage for medical devices, and Microbot's 43-person team must compete against the sales forces of Intuitive Surgical, Siemens, and J&J [2]

Capital exhaustion and dilution — with -$13.1M in net losses and no revenue base, additional equity raises will be necessary to fund commercial operations, diluting existing shareholders in a low share price environment [4]

Competitive response from incumbents — Intuitive Surgical, Siemens (Corindus), and J&J (Monarch) have the resources to develop competing single-use or AI-enhanced endovascular robotic platforms that could undercut LIBERTY's first-mover advantage
